title: External Stakeholder Dependencies — Third-Party SaaS in Auth Flows

compliance.assurance_rationale: NIST CSF GV.OC-02 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST CSF GV.OC-02 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.7.9: External Stakeholder Dependencies — Third-Party SaaS in Auth Flows.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.7.9' running on index=azure sourcetype="mscs:azure:signinlog" (resourceDisplayName, appDisplayName), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110)
spl:
index=azure sourcetype=mscs:azure:signinlog earliest=-30d status.errorCode=0
| stats dc(userPrincipalName) as users, count as signins by appDisplayName, resourceDisplayName
| sort - signins
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src span=1d | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for the Authentication data model (Authentication.Authentication).
description: Surfaces which external identity and SaaS dependencies participate in authentication so organizational context includes supply-side and partner risks.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIST CSF compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes azure and sourcetypes mscs:azure:signinlog. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for External Stakeholder Dependencies — Third-Party SaaS in Auth Flows. The NIST Cybersecurity Framework tells us what good practice looks like, and we keep evidence we match it.
implementation: (1) Scope production tenants; (2) classify appDisplayName via lookup; (3) quarterly review for board risk committee; (4) feed vendor risk register. Indexes required: `index=azure`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=mscs:azure:signinlog`.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=azure` `sourcetype="mscs:azure:signinlog"` (resourceDisplayName, appDisplayName).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Scope production tenants; (2) classify appDisplayName via lookup; (3) quarterly review for board risk committee; (4) feed vendor risk register.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=azure sourcetype=mscs:azure:signinlog earliest=-30d status.errorCode=0
| stats dc(userPrincipalName) as users, count as signins by appDisplayName, resourceDisplayName
| sort - signins
```

Understanding this SPL

**External Stakeholder Dependencies — Third-Party SaaS in Auth Flows (GV.OC-02)** — Surfaces which external identity and SaaS dependencies participate in authentication so organizational context includes supply-side and partner risks.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=azure` `sourcetype="mscs:azure:signinlog"` (resourceDisplayName, appDisplayName). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: azure; **sourcetype**: mscs:azure:signinlog. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=azure, sourcetype=mscs:azure:signinlog, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by appDisplayName, resourceDisplayName** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**External Stakeholder Dependencies — Third-Party SaaS in Auth Flows (GV.OC-02)** — Surfaces which external identity and SaaS dependencies participate in authentication so organizational context includes supply-side and partner risks.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=azure` `sourcetype="mscs:azure:signinlog"` (resourceDisplayName, appDisplayName). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the GRC system, audit register, or framework crosswalk. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table, Bar chart
